title: How to enable restart/resume option. Complete documentation?
description:
Hello, community,

I'm building an automation process around QUICK and attempting to address some execution edge cases. Due to the run-time configuration in the container, long simulations could be interrupted for execution environment reasons. The output file is partially generated and may contain the most recent optimized xyz geometry. 
While it's possible to use the last successful step xyz geometry as a "restart" point to resume the simulation, I found some code references that enable this functionality via execution parameters, although, I was unable to identify those yet. Additionally, I can see these lines in a complete output file confirming this is possible without tricks.

> No restart information is written
> This run has not been restarted.

Could you please point me in the right direction? Also, after looking at the code I have a feeling there's more to the documentation that is published here https://quick-docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user-manual.html, are there any other resources shared elsewhere?

Many thanks in advance!

title: How to interprete QUICK results
description:
Dear Quick Developer team,
I am running QM/MM minimization/single point energy on a set of solvated ligand/protein complexes. 
I would like to ask if you can give me more information on how to correctly interpret the results.
1.	Regarding the quick.out file:
What are the terms contributing to the TOTAL ENERGY? If I compute the sum of the electronic energy, core_core repulsion, dispersion correction, and ext charge repulsion, I don’t retrieve the exact value.
 2.	Regarding the out/log file of the Amber calculation:
What are the terms that contribute to the final value of QUICKESCF? (I guess the same the same as above, in this case the energy is just reported in Hartree)

Many thanks in advance for you time and help.
Best regards,
Laura

title: Github CI Workflow Optimizations
description:
This PR speeds up the serial CPU tests in the Github CI workflow by running those tests in parallel using GNU parallel (`parallel`) in the `runtest` script -- set the variable `PARALLEL_TEST_COUNT` before running `runtest`.  Specifically, use 2 cores for running the serial CPU tests for Github workflow runners.

Furthermore, the ability to run tests is parallel is supported for MPI, single GPU, and MPI+GPU QUICK versions. For MPI and MPI+X versions, set both `PARALLEL_TEST_COUNT` and `DO_PARALLEL`. For GPU versions, set both `PARALLEL_TEST_COUNT` and `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`/`HIP_VISIBLE_DEVICES`, where the number of available GPUs must be at least the number of parallel tests (i.e., at least 1 GPU per each GNU parallel job).

Additional notes/limitations:
- Exporting shell functions for parallel execution with `parallel` required using `bash`-style function exporting.  As such, the default shell used in `runtest` was changed to `bash`.  This should not be a problem with QUICK integration with Amber as many of the test scripts in Amber use `bash`.
-  `parallel`l has the ability to manage the output to STDOUT (see, e.g., `--group`, `--keep-order`).  This is used to keep the test output to STDOUT in-order by test ID.  However, `runtest` also duplicates output to `runtest.log`, which `parallel` cannot manage; as such, simple file locking with spin locks was used to avoid race conditions on writes to this file.  However, while the test outpus are grouped correctly by output per job, the order of job output is not enforced.  As such, the test output order will vary from run to run.  If this becomes problematic for functional reasons, this can be improved in the future.

title: Update Github CI workflows for CPU builds and tests
description:
This MR updates the QUICK tests in Github workflows to use v4 artifact actions  (v1 and v2 deprecated, then removed -- see [here](https://github.blog/changelog/2024-02-13-deprecation-notice-v1-and-v2-of-the-artifact-actions/)).

Additionally, this MR also updates the checkout action (v4), renames the build artifacts to avoid naming conflicts between the two build systems (legacy configure/make, and CMake)., parallelizes the serial builds, switches to invoking the CMake build/install commands directly (instead of depending on the generated Makefiles), and refactoring the general job and step naming to better reflect the tasks.

title: DL-FIND constrained geometry optimization
description:
I have added DL-Find feature for constrained geometry optimization.

```
# Example input for DL-FIND constraint integration in QUICK
HF BASIS=3-21G CONSTRAIN OPTIMIZE

C         -4.13498124  0.70342204  0.00000000
H         -3.53650966  -0.16516720  -0.17967886
H         -4.07172084  1.36057017  -0.84205371
H         -5.15338585  0.41046458  0.14808100
C         -3.62163902  1.42937832  1.25740497
H         -3.68306794  2.48719615  1.10858318
H         -4.22133413  1.15219316  2.09909028
H         -2.60384227  1.15314360  1.43819258

DIHEDRAL 2 1 5 6
```

The syntax for implementing constraints within QUICK closely mirrors established conventions in other QM packages: 

`FREEZEXY atom_no_1`
`DISTANCE atom_no_1 atom_no_2`
`ANGLE atom_no_1 atom_no_2 atom_no_3`
`DIHEDRAL atom_no_1 atom_no_2 atom_no_3 atom_no_4`


Commands such as DISTANCE, ANGLE, and DIHEDRAL enable straightforward specification of constraints, streamlining the optimization process. These constraints operate within the default internal coordinate system (ICOORD=3) of QUICK, simplifying the setup process for users familiar with similar packages.

The freeze atom commands (FREEZE, FREEZEX, FREEZEY, FREEZEZ, FREEZEXY, FREEZEXZ, FREEZEYZ) provide fine-grained control over molecular configurations, enhancing the precision and accuracy of simulations. The freeze atom functionality is compatible with Cartesian coordinate systems (ICOORD=0)of QUICK.

title: HIP and MPI+HIP updates
description:
This MR ports the updated CUDA and MPI+CUDA codes (including recently merged f-function optimizations) to HIP and MPI+HIP versions, respectively.  Also, this MR begins to unify the CUDA and HIP versions to simpily future GPU code maintainence.

Additional work also included in this PR concerns the following items:
- Integer-based atomic operation code paths (used in place of double precision atomics -mainly for older GPUs lacking hardware support [pre-pascal on NVIDIA GPUs])
- Wrappers around CUDA/HIP APIs (better error checking)
- HIP diagonalization (rocsolver re-enabled, CPU code disabled)
- Several bug fixed (memory leaks, initialization errors) 
- ROCm version detection and disabling HIP builds against known buggy ROCm versions (>= v5.4.3)

Limitations / Known Issues:
- f function code (CMake flag `-DENABLEF=TRUE`) does not compile on HIP versions due to resource limitations (stack frame size exceeded in device functions).  Errors are similar to the following (from a build on the AMD Accelerator Cloud platform):

title: Containerizing QUICK: Add Dockerfile and GitHub Actions
description:
This pull request adds a Dockerfile in order to containerize QUICK for use on machines with the x86 architecture and NVIDIA GPUs. The container will have quick, quick.MPI, quick.cuda and quick.cuda.MPI available. This container will run on a single compute node, multi-node operation has not been tested. This container runs on Docker, Singularity and Kubernetes based systems.

The new GitHub Action titled "Docker" will build and publish the QUICK container on the GitHub Container Registry (aka GitHub Packages). Successful builds will be displayed on the packages section of the GitHub repository homepage. The action is triggered for pull requests to the master branch as well as for releases.

title: Fix for ERI gradient performance issue
description:
The enclosed changes resolve #324. There were two problems. 1) I increased the maximum number of primitives (maxprim) per basis function to 20, which was originally 10., 2) For some reason, nvcc takes longer to compile the ERI code when we have the assignment operator (=) instead of the assign-add compound operator (+=) in the VRR code. To lower the compile time, I used += in the VRR code and in the gradient driver, since we have to scale the primitive integrals, I initialized the primitive integral matrix (store2) to zero inside the primitive loops. This is the major culprit for performance hit in the gradient code. I have updated the VRR grad code to use = when saving the primitive integral values into the store2 matrix and eliminated initialization inside primitive loops. The maxprim variable is set to 10 unless we compile the code with F functions.

title: Support for legacy atomics in cuda f gradient kernels
description:
In this PR, I have made changes to CUDA F gradient kernels to support legacy atomic add. Users won't be able to run F kernels on Kepler cards though. This is because we moved device pointers and some constants from constant memory into the shared memory. This improves the performance and the implementation we have in F gradient source files ( https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/326-support-for-legacy-atomics-in-cuda-f-gradient-kernels/src/cuda/gpu_get2e_grad_ffff.cu#L259-L505, https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/326-support-for-legacy-atomics-in-cuda-f-gradient-kernels/src/cuda/gpu_get2e_grad_ffff.cuh#L1695-L1730) is my experimental implementation. We should implement some templates, clean this up, and update other OEI/ERI/LRI kernels to use shared memory. Will be very useful for performance gain on AMD cards. 
I have also reimplemented the ERI sorting algorithm: https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/326-support-for-legacy-atomics-in-cuda-f-gradient-kernels/src/cuda/gpu_get2e_grad_ffff.cu#L167-L251. The old sorting algorithm (https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/326-support-for-legacy-atomics-in-cuda-f-gradient-kernels/src/cuda/gpu.cu#L612-L1137) has a bug and fails for sort high angular momentum ERIs properly. We should move the sorting algorithm I implemented in gpu_get2e_grad_ffff.cu into gpu.cu and do the sorting before the SCF.

title: HIP and MPI+HIP builds broken since adding f-function support (PR #312)
description:
#312 broke all HIP and MPI+HIP builds (with and without f-function support).

New CUDA and MPI+CUDA codes need to be backported to respective HIP and MPI+HIP implementations.

My current working notes on this are as follows:
- Delete all HIP / MPI+HIP sources, and replace with converted CUDA / MPI+CUDA sources using hipify tools
```
cd QUICK/src
rm hip/*.{cu,h,cpp}
rm -rf hip/iclass && cp -r cuda/iclass hip
for FILE in $(ls *.{cu,h,cpp}); do hipify-perl "${FILE}" -o "../hip/${FILE}"; done
```
- Manually fix issues
-- `CUDA_MPIV` -> `HIP_MPIV`
-- src/hip/gpu.cu:49:        `debugFile = fopen("debug.cuda", "w+");`
-- NVTX -> ROC-tracer (https://github.com/ROCm/roctracer)

title: CMake cleans all MPI flags before build
description:
Early this year, I worked to add MPI builds for AmberTools in conda-forge (https://github.com/conda-forge/ambertools-feedstock/pull/133). I got the error `mpi.h: No such file or directory` when compiling QUICK using MPI.

After careful debugging, I found that the following CMake lines clean all compiling flags that are passed by the parent Amber directory, including the path to the MPI directory (something like `-I /path/to/mpi/include`).

https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/fd1ccc4ec0f59ff63f9f74cb895b38c4e3df68b6/CMakeLists.txt#L58-L60

I added a patch to remove the above lines (https://github.com/conda-forge/ambertools-feedstock/pull/133/commits/b9dc271539941373a0b2a54c3e3a70868f2f261e), and now it works fine.

I am unsure whether this patch can be ported to the upstream source code here, as I am unsure what these lines are used for.

title: Unrestricted calculations give wrong results with Intel compiler
description:
UDFT calculations give wrong results with Intel compiler. GNU compiler work.

Following tests fail

- `opt_c2h5rad_b3lyp_631gss`
- `opt_c2hrad_uhf_ccpvdz`
- `opt_merad_b3lyp_631gss`

The output shows wrong number of electrons in the XC quadrature. See https://pastebin.com/Er2avNaE for `grad_ohrad_b3lyp_631gss` which shows 2.5 alpha and 2.5 beta electrons in the XC quadrature. Correct number of electrons is 5 alpha and 4 beta.


Both UHF and UDFT fail. 

Tested on SDSC Expanse A100 nodes with `Intel compilers 19.1.3.304 20200925` and `CUDA 11.2`. Modules loaded as follows
```
module reset
module load intel/19.1.3.304
module load cuda11.7/toolkit
module load openmpi/4.1.3
module load intel-mkl/2020.4.304
module load cmake/3.21.4
```

title: F trap for cEW enabled QM/MM

description: The enclosed changes resolve #333. Please check and merge.

title: Wrong D and F MO coefficient order in QUICK generated .molden files
description:
The MO coefficient section in .molden files written by existing molden exporter in QUICK doesn't follow the correct format for D and F orbitals. The correct format is as follows (ref:  https://www.theochem.ru.nl/molden/molden_format.html).  

```
   5D: D 0, D+1, D-1, D+2, D-2
   6D: xx, yy, zz, xy, xz, yz

   7F: F 0, F+1, F-1, F+2, F-2, F+3, F-3
  10F: xxx, yyy, zzz, xyy, xxy, xxz, xzz, yzz, yyz, xyz
```
This must be fixed ASAP.

title: Fix for issues 323 and 325
description: The enclosed changes fix the segfault in cEW-enabled QM/MM and OEI gradient timing (resolves #323, resolves #325). There was a bug associated with the flag that indicates the availability of F functions. As a result, OEI potential and gradients were computed on the host and the point charge gradient vector wasn't uploaded to the GPU. This caused the segfault. Please check and merge. I will proceed with other issues when this is merged into the master branch.

title: Amber-Quick QM/MM -- QM energies during minimization problem
description:
Hi,

I am trying to run some QM/MM calculations, but the energy minimization of the QM region is being troublesome.

I did a MM minimization of the lipids and solvent molecules of my system (restraining protein atoms and Cu ion) due to lipids always having very bad clashes.

The next minimization is already with QM/MM. I have used basis set def2-SVPD and cc-pVDZ since they can be used for Cu. The initial guess of the QM region looks  good (good energy values, and partial charges). However, when it starts to perform the actual QM calculation, energies and partial charges explode (go to really high values). Can you point at what I am doing wrong or what shoudl I check? This i my first QM/MM simulation, so I don't know where to start.

Thanks for your invaluable help and time.

description:
With support of F functions we should add more basis sets:

description:
1) Add trap to quit with error message if a user tries to use G functions.
2) Remove SAD guess that was made for basis sets / elements with G functions.

description:
Use of `mpif.h` is deprecated. Replace
```
implicit none
include 'mpif.h'
```
with
```
use mpi_f08
implicit none
```

See https://www.mpi-forum.org/docs/mpi-3.1/mpi31-report/node408.htm

title: Add ffunc test
description:
Add grad test with 5 heavy atoms (NMA, N-methylacetamide, RHF/def2-TZVP) so we have a test that covers (ff|ff) ERIs and ERI gradients.

Some commits from PR #314 are in this branch,I think Git should resolve this automatically, if not let me know and I will fix it.

title: Fix opt tests
description:
The optimization tests were not working as intended. Only one geometry optimization test was checking the geometry and only some the dipole moment. The reason was that the awk command in the runtest script in function check_opt() was looking for "OPTIMIZED GEOMETRY INFORMATION" but only one geometry is actually fully optimized to save time. This test fixes this problem and adds new tests.
- Search for "GEOMETRY INFORMATION" in output, which is printed both for fully and partially optimized geometries
- Let merad test fully optimize
- Add DIPOLE keyword where missing
- Update output files accordingly
- Add caffeine B3LYP/3-21G geometry optimization (from Baker test set) so we also test a larger molecule (not in short CPU test list)
- Update some comments in test lists and runtest test description

title: An update to initial information print
description: In this PR, I have updated the print statements that print version and author list into the output file. Specifically, I added pipe symbols into each line to prevent them from being considered for test vs reference output file comparisons in the test suite. This should fix QUICK test failures due to version mismatches in the AmberTools test suite.

title: Enable support for F functions
description:
This PR enables support for F functions in the ERI and ERI gradient calculations.

Limitations include
- OEIs are computed on CPU if F functions are present
- Open shell gradients with F functions do not work on GPU
- CUDA F implementation requires a lot of device scratch memory. Calculations will fail on GPUs with little memory (e.g. < 16GB RAM)
- ERI sorting algorithm for F kernels is not optimal
- HIP and HIPMPI is not tested

For details see PR #299 

Following needs to be done (can be done in separate PR)
- Add trap for G functions
- Add trap for open shell + F functions + gradient + CUDA
- Add test with 4 centers with f functions (e.g. def2-TZVP basis)

title: QUICK on Perlmutter (NERSC)
description:
Hi, 

It seems that the CMake build system for QUICK fails to build correctly on the Perlmutter computer at NERSC. This is due to the use of the outdated `find_package(CUDA)` option being in used. This variable used to populate correctly the `CUDA_CUBLAS_LIBRARY` variable, however the path to the library in the HPC toolkit of nvidia seems to different, instead of being in: `/path/to/cuda/lib64/libcublas` it is in `/path/to/math_libs/lib64/libcublas.so` same for `cusolver`. 

The temporary workaround was to pass the full path `import_library(cublas "/opt/nvidia/hpc_sdk/Linux_x86_64/22.7/math_libs/lib64/libcublas.so")` in the QUICKCudaConfig.cmake file. This is using cmake 3.22.0 if this helps at all.

title: Support for F functions
description:
In this PR, I have enabled support for f functions in the ERI and ERI gradient calculations. Specifically, the following tasks were performed. 
1. Updated error trap for F functions. The trap activates only if the user compiles the code without F function support.
2. Debugged F subroutines of legacy CPU ERI code. 
3. Implemented gradient calculation of ERIs containing F in CPU code. 
4. Ported 2 and 3 implementations to GPU.
5. Implemented parallel versions of 2-4. 
6. Enabled support to compile new source files added in 3-5 in CMake build system.
7. Performance optimization of F kernels in CUDA and CUDAMPI versions. 
8. Updated default basis set collection with cc-pVTZ, def2-tZVP and 6-311G(2df,2pd). Also generated atomic densities required for the SAD guess of these basis sets. 
9. Updated test suite with energy, gradient, and geometry optimization tests with cases containing F functions. 
10. Updated CI to compile the code with F function support and test. 

The accuracy of the implementations (energy and gradients) was tested against a reference software. The results were in excellent agreement. Please check the PR and test exhaustively. 

The code can be configured using the CMake build system for Volta architecture with F function support and GNU compiler toolchain as follows (assuming the build directory is located inside QUICK home directory). 

`cmake .. -DMPI=TRUE -DCUDA=TRUE -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=$(pwd)/../install -DCOMPILER=GNU -DQUICK_USER_ARCH=volta -DENABLEF=TRUE`

Here is an accuracy and performance comparison of PSB3 gradient calculation at the B3LYP/cc-pVTZ level of theory. CUDA tests were run on NVIDIA A100 cards.

The input and output files of these runs can be found inside the example.tar.gz attached below. 

Finally, it is worth noticing the limitations of the current CUDA/CUDAMPI F implementations. 
- CUDA one electron integral code does not support F functions. If a calculation contains F functions, CPU OEI implementation will be used. (Eg. https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/ffunc/src/modules/quick_oei_module.f90#L113-L130)
- CUDA F implementation requires a lot of device scratch memory (https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/ffunc/src/cuda/gpu.cu#L244-L282). Therefore, calculations will fail to run on cards having low amounts of device memory (Eg. cards with < 16 GB memory). 
- The ERI sorting algorithm used for F kernels is not optimal (https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/ffunc/src/cuda/gpu.cu#L606-L610). An optimized version has been implemented in the Gen3 ERI effort which is currently WIP. 
- QUICK/Amber QM/MM has not been tested with F functions. 
- Support for open shell calculations is not available. 
- HIP and HIPMPI versions haven't been tested.

title: Basis set superposition error?
description:
Most of time I spend optimizing geometry of hydrogen-bonded complexes, like DNA basepairs. B3LYP+D3 / cc-pVDZ is very good mathod for that as long as counterpointwise corrections of basis-set superposition error is applied. Without counterpointwise correction much large basis set (like aug-cc-pVTZ) is required to achieve comparable accuracy.

On [this page](http://vergil.chemistry.gatech.edu/active_bfdb/bfdb/cgi-bin/comparisonviewer.py) can be easily compared different methods and basisets which shows how importaint this correction is for weakly bonded complexes. I also attached plot for hydrogen-bonded complexes from S22 dataset (CP is with correction, unCP is without correction).

- Is that any possibility to do some BSSE correton with QUICK? 
- Idea would be apply this correction during geometry optimization so that optimized geometry of the dimer is also correct.
   - For example in Psi4 there is [this function](https://psicode.org/psi4manual/master/nbody.html) for that.
- if there is not way how to do BSSE correction with QUICK, is there some possibility to use at least aug-cc-pVDZ basiset?

![Page 1](https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/assets/3408568/1a9da901-4269-421e-aa1b-238304c6d640)

title: AWG: Remove redundant hard-coded SCF DIIS exit condition
description:
The exit condition `idiis.gt.MAX_DII_CYCLE_TIME*quick_method%maxdiisscf` is 1) redundant in the current implementation and 2) leads to a hard-coded maximum number of SCF iterations (DIIS subspace size times hardcoded number of maximum DIIS iterations). This is dumb, also because `idiis` is always equal `jscf`, hence it is sufficient to check for `jscf` as exit condition to limit the number of SCF iterations.

Here we remove the redundant condition and get rid of unnecessary `MAX_DII_CYCLE_TIME`. The user can choose the number of SCF iterations and size of DIIS subspace. There is no need to limit the number of DIIS cycles.

title: Add options for initial guess and refactor SCF convergence algorithms
description:
In general the DIIS based SCF convergence with SAD guess implemented in QUICK works well and is efficient.

In some rare instances of specific combinations of molecules and basis sets the SCF can converge to a solution that differs from other quantum chemistry codes. We have seen this for cytosine HF and B3LYP single point energies with the STO-3G basis set and the cc-pVDZ basis set (not other basis sets) that matched some versions but not other versions of other codes. At this point it is not clear whether QUICK and/or which versions of other codes converge to the correct or wrong solution.

In any event we should provide additional options for initial guess generation
- core Hamiltonian guess (not a good guess but nice to have as reference)
- superposition of atomic potentials (SAP)

and revisit the SCF algorithms
- refactor / clean up
- add second order SCF

title: Some improvements to AMD version
description:
This PR contains following improvements. 

1. Capability to pass architecture flags in AMD version. For eg. QUICK can be configured for MI2XX using CMake build system as follows. 
`cmake .. -DHIP=TRUE -DMPI=TRUE -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=$(pwd)/../install -DCOMPILER=GNU -DHIP_TOOLKIT_ROOT_DIR=$ROCM_PATH -DMAGMA=TRUE -DMAGMA_ROOT=~/magma-2.6.2/install-gfx90a -DQUICK_USER_ARCH=gfx90a `
Currently only gfx90a and gfx908 architecture flags are supported. The code will be configured and compiled for MI100 (gfx908) if no architecture is specified. 
2. Tested and set optimal kernel launching parameters for MI2XX. Enabled double precision atomic adds in MI2XX version.
3. Updated API to pass flag to reuse density matrix, integral cutoff, density matrix convergence threshold and XC cutoff.

Please merge this PR only after merging PRs #259 , #263, #264 and #265.

title: Protect the loop exit condition with MAX_DII_CYCLE
description:
With the current setup, the SCF loop is interrupted after 300 iterations even when `SCF=VALUE` key contains `VALUE` more than 300 (tried with 1000).

The 300 is the result of `MAX_DII_CYCLE_TIME*quick_method%maxdiisscf`, where `MAX_DII_CYCLE_TIME` hardcoded value is 30 and the default `maxdiisscf` value is 10.

This PR fixes this issue so that the current loop break condition is only checked when the total number of DIIS cycles (I  named it `MAX_DII_CYCLE`) is more than the max number of SCF cycles.

Alternatively, the control of the `MAX_DII_CYCLE_TIME` can be given to the user by supplying the corresponding input keyword. Let me know if this might be of interest.

**Note:** I have only patched the conventional SCF module. The same fix can be applied in `quick_uscf_module.f90` and `quick_sad_guess_module.f90` modules.

title: Differences in the HF energies for the cc-pVDZ basis set
description:
Hello QUICK,

First of all, thank you for all the hard work you put into this software!

I have been running some Hartree-Fock benchmark calculations for different basis sets and have observed that the HF/cc-pVDZ results for some molecules differ significantly from the reference codes. Below is a brief description of the software stack:
- QUICK running in a container, installed from the latest [release tag 22.03](https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/releases/tag/QUICK-22.03). In fact, I also tried QUICK build from the latest commit ac19bfe4749dbe83d76b93125e3f7c5afda4beca and with more tight cutoffs for different quantities and I keep getting the same value. I also tried to regenerate the SAD guess and recalculate the HF energy from it, but the result did not change much
- Psi4 (release 1.3.2, Git: Rev {HEAD} ecbda83) 
- GAMESS (release 17.09-r2) [container from the NVIDIA catalog](https://catalog.ngc.nvidia.com/orgs/hpc/containers/gamess)

Compiled and run on x86_64 machines with A100 (cluster) and T550 (laptop) NVIDIA GPUs.

**Note 1:** I used a water molecule geometry different from the one that you have in the unit test [here](https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/master/test/ene_H2O_rhf_ccpvdz.in).
**Note 2:** results computed with Pople basis sets are in perfect agreement with the reference codes (errors within $10^{-8}$ Hartree).

Below I am reporting the HF/cc-pVDZ energies in Hartree for a water molecule (same geometry has been used for all calculations):

| QUICK   |      Psi4      |  GAMESS |
|----------|:-------------:|------|
| -76.026199736 |  -76.025888236 | -76.025888241 |

**Question:** is it expected to have the mHartree differences for larger basis sets? 

The QUICK input is the following:
```
 HF BASIS=cc-pVDZ cutoff=1.0e-9 denserms=1.0e-8 ENERGY
 
 O  -0.06756756   -0.31531531    0.00000000  
 H   0.89243244   -0.31531531    0.00000000  
 H  -0.38802215    0.58962052    0.00000000  
```

If I use the water molecule geometry from the aforementioned unit test, the results are slightly better but still within $10^{-4}$:

| QUICK   |      Psi4      |  GAMESS |
|----------|:-------------:|------|
| -75.999485993|   -75.999131653 | -75.999131682 |

title: Support for MAGMA in AMD version
description:
I have made necessary changes to make use of dsyevd diagonalizer form MAGMA library in AMD version. MAGMA must be installed on the target platform. Users will have to pass `-DMAGMA=TRUE` and set `-DMAGMA_ROOT=magma_path` (or set MAGMA_ROOT environment variable or have magma.so in LD_LIBRARY_PATH) when configuring cmake for HIP. 
Performance w.r.t. lapack dsyevd is system dependent. For taxol b3lyp/6-31G** example (1160 basis functions), lapack dsyevd diagonalization takes ~20 s for 11 SCF iterations. MAGMA dsyevd on MI100 takes ~8 s. 
I havent enabled magma support for CUDA version. This can be done in another PR if necessary. Please merge PR #259 and #263 before merging this into master.

title: Support for AMD GPUs
description:
I have enabled support for AMD GPUs in this PR. On a platform with an AMD gpu and rocm-4.5 or above installed, one can configure serial and multi-GPU versions using legacy build as follows.

```
./configure --hip gnu (gnu compiler assumed)
./configure --hipmpi gnu
```

The above will generate make.hip.in and running make will compile the code for architecture of the available GPU resulting in quick.hip/ quick.hip.mpi executables. It is also possible to specify an architecture with --arch flag. For MI50 and MI100 this would be --arch gfx906 and --arch gfx908 respectively. I haven't tested on other architectures.

To configure and build QUICK using CMake build system, following steps can be followed.

```
cmake .. -DHIP=TRUE -DMPI=TRUE -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=$(pwd)/../install -DCOMPILER=GNU
make -j16 install
```

The performance of current version of the code on AMD GPUs is ~1.5-2x slower than on a NVIDIA card with similar specs (based on MI100 vs V100 taxol B3LYP/6-31G** gradient time comparison). This is mainly due to the higher register spillage of device kernels on AMD cards and the use of host diagonalizer for matrix diagonalization. The rocSolver diagonalizer is currently inefficient: https://github.com/ROCmSoftwarePlatform/rocSOLVER/issues/353 and the corresponding calls to DSYEVD should be enabled once it is improved. I have already implemented the necessary driver.
Furthermore, double precision atomic add is surprisingly inefficient on AMD cards. Therefore, I have set the integer based legacy atomic add as default for now.

title: GPU porting of new grid weight derivative algorithm

After optimizations, the new implementation is several times faster than old less accurate algorithm (eg. 478 ms vs 3.26 s kernel time for Taxol B3LYP/STO-3G test on Titan V). The new kernel only consumes ~110 registers per thread. The performance can be further improved by increasing the number of threads per block. I have set this to 256 for now because the size of a temporary device array  depends on it: https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/sswder/src/cuda/gpu.cu#L2144 . Increasing this too much may cause problems when running calculations of large molecular systems on cards with low device memory. We should increase the tpb after a systematic study. 
I have also made changes to call Tim's grid weight derivative implementation in CPU version.

title: DFT-D3 integrated QUICK
description:
@agoetz I have integrated DFT-D3 library (https://www.chemie.uni-bonn.de/pctc/mulliken-center/software/dft-d3/get-the-current-version-of-dft-d3) into QUICK. The specific keywords to invoke dispersion correction are as follows. 

D2 - Use DFT-D2 version, same as `-old` option in DFT-D3 program (see page 5 of this manual: https://www.chemie.uni-bonn.de/pctc/mulliken-center/software/dft-d3/man.pdf)
D3 - DFT-D3 with zero damping, same as `-zero` option in DFT-D3 program
D3BJ - DFT-D3 with Becke-Johnson damping, same as `-bj` option in DFT-D3 program
D3M - Use modified zero damping by Sherrill and coworkers, same as `-zerom` option in DFT-D3 program 
D3MBJ - Use modified Becke-Johnson damping by Sherrill and coworkers, same as `-bjm` option in DFT-D3 program 

An example job card for a B3LYP/DEF2-SVP gradient calculation with D3BJ is as follows. 

`B3LYP D3BJ BASIS=DEF2-SVP cutoff=1.0e-9 denserms=1.0e-6 CHARGE=+1 GRADIENT DIPOLE`

Preliminary benchmarks with software XXX shows that energies and gradients resulting from all four QUICK versions (serial, mpi, cuda, cudampi) are correct. Please test and let me know if we need any changes.

title: Support for AMD GPUs
description:
I have enabled support for AMD GPUs in this PR. On a platform with an AMD gpu and rocm-4.5 or above installed, one can configure serial and multi-GPU versions using legacy build as follows. 

./configure --hip gnu (gnu compiler assumed)
./configure --hipmpi gnu 

The above will generate make.hip.in and running `make` will compile the code for architecture of the available GPU resulting in quick.hip/ quick.hip.mpi executables. It is also possible to specify an architecture with --arch flag. For MI50 and MI100 this would be --arch gfx906 and --arch gfx908 respectively. I haven't tested on other architectures. 

The performance of current version of the code on AMD GPUs is ~1.5-2x slower than on a NVIDIA card with similar specs (based on MI100 vs V100 taxol B3LYP/6-31G** gradient time comparison). This is mainly due to the higher register spillage of device kernels on AMD cards and the use of host diagonalizer for matrix diagonalization. The rocSolver diagonalizer is currently inefficient: https://github.com/ROCmSoftwarePlatform/rocSOLVER/issues/353 and the corresponding calls to DSYEVD should be enabled once it is improved. I have already implemented the necessary driver.  

Things remaining to do: 
1. Fixing CMake build
2. Some cleanups (eg. use of common preprocessor flags in place CUDA and HIP specific flags and etc)
3. Enabling QM/MM support with AMBER
4. Testing on different AMD GPUs and updating configure script with arch flags and etc. 
5. Revisiting performance tuning once ERI code is refactored

Task 1 and 3 will require linking all objects with hipcc compiler since device objects need a special treatment: https://github.com/ROCm-Developer-Tools/HIP/issues/2406. We should keep this in mind.

title: Release QUICK binaries for serial, MPI and CUDA
description:
Hi,
i tried to run QUICK, but there are no binaries for MPI, serial or CUDA. Most quantum chemical packages release binaries
to allow researchers to actually use the program. Many of the larger packages release 5-10 binaries, others just one, that 
can handle all the architectures. I myself actually do not want to compile QUICK, but rather use it.

So I wonder if there are any plans to release QUICK binaries for LINUX. I agree there are many configurations possible, but
it should be possible to have some backwards compatibility and working binaries. Unless that is not wanted. 
Thank you

description:
Hi,
I have installed quick via minconda (AmberTools21) but I am missing any examples
that will finish within a couple of minutes. I went here but there are no examples 
how to use **QUICK** and **sander** in a QM/MM approach 
(https://quick-docs.readthedocs.io/en/21.3.0/hands-on-tutorials.html)

The delta density based SCF works for HF but the energy was wrong because the energy was computed with the difference density and not the true total density.

The delta density based SCF is still broken for DFT.

title: Light and Shadow in a Windows port with CUDA support (using MSVC and Intel Fortran)
description:
Dear QUICK Team,

I did a Windows port with CUDA support (see https://github.com/MSoegtropIMC/QUICK/tree/windows-cmake). As far as I understand this dows not work with MinGW because according to nVidia nvcc supports only Microsoft Visual C as C compiler with CUDA on Windows - see [Cuda Windows](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-installation-guide-microsoft-windows/index.html). So in short I did the following:

- use Microsoft Visual Studio 2019 as C/C++ compiler
- use Intel Fortan Classic as Fortran compiler
- use Intel MKL for BLAS, LAPACK, ...
- use Ninja as cmake build backend
- a few compatibilitiy changes
  - created a Fortran module to interface the C functions using Fortran 90's standardized mechanism for this
  - replaced variable length arrays with alloca (MSVC does not support variable length arrays)
  - added a few dllexport declaration to C and Fortran
  - fixed a few minor header incompatibilities
 
The good news: I can compile all 4 variants (with and without MPI/Cuda) and they run (tstes only the base and the CUDA variant so far). I can reproduce the test results in a few of teh advanced cases (did not test all as yet) for energy, gradient and optimization runs with very minor deviations.

But I also see a few odd effects:

- I cannot reproduce the gradients from the hands on tutorial section "HF/DFT gradient calculation) [Tutorial](https://quick-docs.readthedocs.io/en/21.3.0/hands-on-tutorials.html). What I get is just completely different. Is it possible that the data in the tutorial is wrong? I find it rather odd that more complicated test cases work and this simple case doesn't. I attached my input and output file.
[Gradient.zip](https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/files/6721251/Gradient.zip)

- I did run an optimization run for C60 with B3LYP DFT and Dunning cc-pVDZ basis and there is really odd stuff going on in the initial SCF convergence run. After 40 iterations which look reasonable, the energy suddenly drops from -2246 to -4865 and then keeps dropping in large steps from time to time, e.g. from -4639 to -10443 at iteration 48. As far as I can tell an energy around -2250...-2260 is correct. I attached the input and output file of the run. It would be great if you could check if you see the same effect or not. This is a CUDA run (I didn't try the normal version).
[C60.zip](https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/files/6721246/C60.zip)

One last thing: do you in general appreciate this activity, even if it means to do some minor compatibiliyt changes to the code, or are you already more than busy enough with Linux? I would say the port was quite smooth after I found a combination of compilers which works. Using a Fortran module to interface C - see [quick_c_interface.f90](https://github.com/MSoegtropIMC/QUICK/blob/windows-cmake/src/modules/quick_c_interface.f90) - is I think in general a good idea because it is less error prone and the other changes are quite minor, although the VLA->alloca change is a bit ugly.

title: MPI build fails in CI

`$installdir/lib/$buildtype` directories and uses names `libquick.so`, `libquick-mpi.so`, etc.

while the CMake build uses

Module file location also seems to be inconsistent (in subdirectories of `$installdir/include`)

title: Bug in MPI code
description:
The following error is occasionally observed when quick.MPI is launched with a single process. 

```
mpirun -np 1 quick.MPI foo.in 
--------------------------------------------------------------------------
mpirun has exited due to process rank 0 with PID 0 on
node louie exiting improperly. There are three reasons this could occur:
1. this process did not call "init" before exiting, but others in
the job did. This can cause a job to hang indefinitely while it waits
for all processes to call "init". By rule, if one process calls "init",
then ALL processes must call "init" prior to termination.
2. this process called "init", but exited without calling "finalize".
By rule, all processes that call "init" MUST call "finalize" prior to
exiting or it will be considered an "abnormal termination"
3. this process called "MPI_Abort" or "orte_abort" and the mca parameter
orte_create_session_dirs is set to false. In this case, the run-time cannot
detect that the abort call was an abnormal termination. Hence, the only
error message you will receive is this one.
This may have caused other processes in the application to be
terminated by signals sent by mpirun (as reported here).
You can avoid this message by specifying -quiet on the mpirun command line.
```

title: Improve CMake build system
description:
The current build system is based on the CMake build system in Amber. It should be simplified and made independent of Amber. Following points need improvement
- Remove cmake code that is unrelated to Quick
- Put code from quick-cmake into cmake
- Move compiler flag definitions from root CMakeLists.txt into cmake/CompilerFlags.cmake

This must be coordinated with including Quick as ExternalPackage in Amber. Currently, Quick inherits Amber's build flags when built within Amber. This is also the reason why we need to overwrite compiler flags in the root CMakeLists.txt.

See also discussion in PR #149

title: Error handling improvements
description: This PR contains changes necessary to propagate and handle exceptions properly in QUICK. Specifically, I have introduced `ierr` variable to subroutine calls (eg. https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/main.f90#L63), throw exceptions from appropriate places by assigning integer values to ierr variable (eg. https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/subs/quick_open.f90#L84. The exception are caught by CHECK_ERROR() or SAFE_CALL() macros (eg. https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/main.f90#L64, https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/main.f90#L60) which are defined in a header file: https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/util/util.fh#L35-L48. Exception handling is done inside a new module: https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/modules/quick_exception_module.f90. @vwcruzeiro Note that I have also added `ierr` variables to API and you will have to update the function calls from Sander (see here for eg: https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/quick_api_test.f90#L68, https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/quick_api_test.f90#L104, https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/quick_api_test.f90#L116, https://github.com/Madu86/QUICK/blob/fix85/src/quick_api_test.f90#L136). These changes should help us to handle most important exceptions in QUICK at the moment; but of course we will have to further improve this implementation. I think this is a good foundation. Let me know your suggestions.

title: New tests
description:
I have updated the test suite with new test cases. Specifically, I have added elementary tests for STO-3G, 3-21G, 6-31G, 6-31G*, 6-31G**, cc-pVDZ, def2-svp, def2-svpd basis sets. These are just HF energy calculations and the goal is to make sure that compounds containing H, Li, Be, B, C, N, O, F, Na, Mg, Al, Si, P, S, Cl, K, Ca, Br, I elements will run SCF. The compounds I have selected are AlH3, BH3, BeH2, CaCal2, CH4, HBr, H2O, KCl, LiF, Ki, MgO, NH4, NaCl, PH3/PO4-3, SO2 and SiH4. 

For 6-31G, 6-31G* and 6-31G** basis sets,  CaCal2, HBr and KCl SAD guess take too long (> 1 min). For cc-pvDZ AlH3, MgO, PH3, SiH4, SO2, CaCl2, HBr, KCl, NaCl take too long. For def2-svpd CaCl2, HBr and HI take too long. Therefore I have temporarily disabled them. @Altaimorin is currently working on saving SAD guess. We can enable these tests when his implementation is in place. 

Apart from the above tests, I have added new point charge gradient tests and geometry optimization tests. @agoetz @vwcruzeiro Please check and let me know what else we need. 

@vwcruzeiro Can we extract energies and  gradients from reference outputs when we run amber test script? I see you have saved reference files for individual components. In this way we won't keep files with duplicate information inside the saved folder.

title: def2-SV(P) basis set is missing
description:
Someone please add the def2-SV(P) basis set. Available on https://www.basissetexchange.org

As an FYI, basis set equivalents are approximately
6-31G* = def2-SV(P)
6-31G** = def2-SVP
6-31G++G(d,p) = def2-SVPD

Only that def2 basis sets are much better (closer to basis set limit for same size).

title: Error trap for f functions
description:
This PR contains an error trap to prevent users from using f functions and should fix issue #83. 

I have done this by implementing a new error handling scheme. Specifically, I have added a new module (quick_exception_module.f90, contains a couple of subroutines related to error handling) and a fortran header file (util.fh, contains some macro definitions). 
To handle new exceptions, developers must include util.fh in fortran source file and use quick_exception_module in subroutines. The error code and message must be set in the case statement in print_exception subroutine. The error code must be thrown from an appropriate place of a subroutine and caught using CHECK_ERROR() macro immediately after calling that subroutine. 
Note that exception handling will be disabled in debug/debug-time modes. This is important for stack tracing and debugging using debuggers.

title: Error trap for energy calculations with f functions in CUDA version

title: Improvements to Boys function
description:
This PR contains updates to Boys function and should fix issue #64 in serial and MPI versions. Specifically, I have updated the cutoff for using upward recurrence relations in FmT subroutine (and FmT device kernel). The cutoff was determined by fitting Boys function values from QUICK FmT subroutine to the values from MIRP boys_exact function.
I have also added the option to link and use MIRP Boys function in the build system. MIRP library and dependencies must be compiled and MIRP_HOME, MIRP_DEP_HOME must be set before configuring QUICK. The option currently works only with --shared option.

title: Fix77: integrate MKL/LAPACK subroutines
description:
This branch is to integrate MKL and LAPACK subroutines into QUICK as an option for diagonalizations. Users need to load MKL or OPENBLAS modules so that $MKLROOT or $EBROOTOPENBLAS can be detected, otherwise the conventional subroutine for diagonalizations will be used. No library paths need be specified for linking the librarys.

Example for compilation on ICER HPCC:
module purge
module load intel/2020a    (imkl is loaded as a part of the toolchain)
make distclean
./configure --mkl intel

export QUICK_INSTALL=/path/to/QUICK/installation

title: Improvements for DIIS
description:
This pull request contains an improvement to diis of cuda and cudampi versions. The formation of the density matrix becomes expensive as the number of basis functions goes up so I have ported the corresponding piece to GPU. The observed times are as follows (left and right are old and new respectively).
1) water B3LYP/cc-pvDZ, 25 basis functions: No significant improvement 
      TOTAL DII TIME      =     0.567264000(  8.20%)	      |	      TOTAL DII TIME      =     0.560116000(  8.03%)
       TOTAL DIAG TIME    =     0.309894000(  4.48%)     |	            TOTAL DIAG TIME    =     0.308439000(  4.42%)

2) water30 B3LYP/def2-svp, 750 basis functions: Small improvement
      TOTAL DII TIME      =     4.267836000(  7.94%)	      |	      TOTAL DII TIME      =     3.670340000(  6.91%)
            TOTAL DIAG TIME    =     0.852865000(  1.59%)     |	            TOTAL DIAG TIME    =     0.847015000(  1.59%)

3) Taxol B3LYP/6-31G**, 1160 basis functions: Small improvement (see here for full comparison: https://pastebin.com/vjRvHvtA)
      TOTAL DII TIME      =    17.012937000(  4.39%)	      |	      TOTAL DII TIME      =    13.589752000(  3.54%)
            TOTAL DIAG TIME    =     1.646698000(  0.42%)     |	            TOTAL DIAG TIME    =     1.649029000(  0.43%)

4) Olestra B3LYP/6-31G**, 4015 basis functions: ~60% improvement (see here for full comparison: https://pastebin.com/Mfp5FB8f)
      TOTAL DII TIME      =   570.893714000( 39.06%)	      |	      TOTAL DII TIME      =   197.258320000( 18.17%)
            TOTAL DIAG TIME    =    14.717386000(  1.01%)     |	            TOTAL DIAG TIME    =    14.802130000(  1.36%)

The new implementation is better for the cuda and cudampi versions. In the olestra example, the formation of density matrix during a given SCF iteration takes about 21 s using the old implementation. This becomes ~0.5 s using the new.

title: Updates for AMBER compatible install and uninstall
description:
I have made the following changes to configure script. 
1) Removed --amprefix and introduced --amber flag. 

This new flag requires the user to specify AMBER_HOME as --prefix. So one can configure for AMBER compatible QUICK installation as follows.
  `./configure --serial --mpi --cuda --cudampi --arch volta --shared --amber --prefix $AMBER_HOME gnu`

The script will verify the $AMBER_HOME by checking if AmberTools directory exist inside the given prefix.

2) Modified Makefiles to eliminate aminstall, amunistall dependency 
After the configuration, the user should run `make` and then `make install` to install executables  and libraries into bin and lib folders in $AMBER_HOME. We will check for the existence of bin and lib directories during `make install`. Uninstallation is done by running `make uninstall`.

title: QUICK library installation into amber lib directory
description:
This pull request contains modifications to copy quick libraries into $AMBERHOME/lib folder. Running `make aminstall` will now copy the following libraries. 
Serial version:
    libblas-quick.so  libquick.so  libxc.so

mpi version:
    libblas-quick.so  libquick-mpi.so  libxc.so

cuda version:
    libquick-cuda.so  libxc-cuda.so

cudampi version:
    libquick-cudampi.so  libxc-cuda.so

Running `make amuninstall` will remove them from $AMBERHOME/lib folder.

title: updates to install quick executables in AMBER installation directory
description:
These updates enable installing and uninstalling quick executables in $AMBER_HOME/bin. To install QUICK from AMBER scripts, (assuming serial installation) do the following.
`./configure --serial --amprefix $AMBER_HOME gnu`
`make`
`make aminstall`
To uninstall, simply run `make amuninstall`

2.Choose either `INTEL` or `GNU` as the _compiler_ for build:

The executables should be found in the `bin` folder.

title: My take on a CMake build system
description:
So I was already about 75% done with implementing a CMake build system when I got Dr. Goetz's email saying that Chi's one was already finished.  Oh well... I figured I'd post what I had anyway so you guys can look it over.  This build system is able to compile and link `quick`, `quick.mpi`, and `quick.cuda`.  However, I haven't tested them yet so I don't know if they run properly.  

I wrote this build system using the library of CMake scripts I developed for Amber.  This library is designed to be used in other programs and you can turn parts of it on and off as needed by including the relevant scripts.  I've already used it to create the standalone build systems for other Amber programs (cpptraj and cphstats).  Yeah it includes a fair amount of extra code files that aren't needed here, but it isn't doing any harm and we can delete them later if needed.  However, this library does provide quite a bit of useful functionality, including:
- Finding MPI and creating targets for it, as well as the adding the `make_mpi_version()` function
- Finding CUDA
- Finding BLAS/MKL and giving an option to switch between them and the internal blas
- Automatically providing correct compile flags for all of Amber's supported compilers

So, it does save quite a lot of work to use this library!  I've gotten into a pretty good groove of using it to hammer out scientific computing build systems as it handles a lot of the hard stuff for you.  Also, I had already wrote a build system for the version of QUICK in the Amber repository, so I was able to draw on that (very old) work to some extent.

Also, this build system is designed to work equally well inside and outside Amber.  It's set up so that it can be directly added as a subdirectory by AmberTools, and it will use the flags and information already present in the amber build system.  If it's not an Amber subdirectory (INSIDE_AMBER = FALSE), then it loads the amber build system library itself.  Importantly, this build system uses Amber's methodology of pan-configuration builds: it doesn't _switch_ to MPI or CUDA versions, it just copies the serial version of the program target and creates a parallel version with MPI or CUDA enabled.   Hopefully this can save you a lot of work when testing the program as you can use one build directory to test all four variants (serial, mpi, cuda, and cudampi).  I've found this method also helps to prevent build system errors to some extent since you have to be more aware of what changes are needed to make the MPI/CUDA version.

Anyway, hope this is useful at least as a reference.  I can explain about it some more at the meeting tomorrow.
Oh also, the pull request for this is showing a bunch of unimportant changes due to me adding the amber cmake-buildscripts, so it's hard to see the actual new code.  I put all the new buildscripts into a separate commit, you can view them [here](https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/commit/080ab85894ecd3dbe689e7d804d24743142e6d98).

title: Not able to build on Mac
description:
I got this error message when I try to build on Mac
```
ld: warning: ignoring file ./lib/blas.a, file was built for archive which is not the architecture being linked (x86_64): ./lib/blas.a
Undefined symbols for architecture x86_64:
  "_dgemm_", referenced from:
      _electdiis_ in scf.o
ld: symbol(s) not found for architecture x86_64
```

This looks like BLAS does not have right make arguments for x86_64. A hacky way is to modify
``src/BLAS/make.inc``

Change
```
ARCH     = ar
ARCHFLAGS= cr
```
To
```
ARCH     = libtool
ARCHFLAGS= -static -o
```

title: Few bugs in MPI version of the HF geometry optimization
description:
Hi all, 
I have detected and fixed following bugs to get the MPI version of HF gradient code to work. 

1) https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/master/src/gradient.f90#L421 
The master computes the energy weighted density matrix  and stores in HOLD array. The values are not broadcasted but slaves are supposed to use them on L523. 
Fix: Broadcast the computed HOLD array 

2)https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/master/src/mpi_setup.f90#L160, https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/master/src/mpi_setup.f90#L161  The size of the arrays (6*nbasis) is incorrect. The arrays won’t be broadcasted.
Fix: Use the correct values

3)https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/master/src/shell.f90#L1811
quick_molspec%chg is not broadcasted. Therefore, slaves will assume zero values 
In MPI version. 
Fix: Broadcast quick_molspec%chg from mpi_setup.f90

4)https://github.com/merzlab/QUICK/blob/master/src/optimize.f90#L82
MPI geometry optimization would fail at L82 after the first iteration. This is because slave nodes do not have access to quick_method%iopt value. 
Fix: Broadcast quick_method%iopt from mpi_setup.f90

With regards,
Madu
